design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate grid-based maze levels, eliminate Nazi enemies, manage ammunition/health resources, reach elevator or defeat boss to progress
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Survival resource management (ammo, health) against waves of enemies in claustrophobic grid-based environments; tension between exploration for secrets and reaching exit
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Allied spy infiltrating Nazi strongholds, eliminating enemy combatants, discovering hidden caches and secret rooms
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Pioneering fast-paced FPS action philosophy: deliberately violent and visceral unlike contemporary games; emphasizes speed and technical prowess over stealth (despite source material). Strict grid-based level design with equal-height walls creates uniform tactical space. Shareware distribution model as design choice—first episode free to drive sales

difficulty.lives_system: Player begins each episode with 4 lives; loses one life and restarts level when health reaches zero; can gain additional lives through high scores or finding extra-life tokens
difficulty.continues_system: Level-restart system upon death; original DOS version allows save-anywhere, but many ports restrict saving between levels only
difficulty.checkpointing: Checkpoint at level boundaries; death resets current level. Original version permits mid-level saves; ported versions (including Genesis) typically restrict to inter-level checkpoints
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player controls weapon selection and movement pace; no difficulty settings documented; progression mandatory after boss defeat but optional secret-room exploration allows skill expression through completion percentage and speedrun times

progression.upgrade_system: Weapon progression: knife → pistol → submachine gun → chain gun (rapid-fire). No stat upgrades; weapon acquisition is level-dependent pickup system
progression.unlock_mechanism: Keys found in levels unlock specific doors; progression requires finding correct path and defeating boss. Secret areas unlocked by discovering hidden wall triggers
replayability_hooks: Completion percentage tracking (enemies killed, treasures collected, secrets found)
Extra-life scoring system incentivizes high-score chasing
Secret rooms with hidden treasures and supplies reward exploration

world_building_techniques: Level-to-level theming: Nazi bunkers and buildings establish setting
Episode naming reflects narrative progression ('Escape from Castle Wolfenstein', 'Operation: Eisenfaust', 'Die, Führer, Die!')
Environmental design (Nazi aesthetics, prison setting, military architecture) conveys antagonist faction without cutscenes

boss_design.philosophy: Each episode concludes with single 'particularly difficult enemy' boss fight. Bosses serve as mandatory progression gates and difficulty peaks within linear structure. Pattern-based design suited to 2.5D sprite-based technical constraints
notable_bosses: High-ranking Nazi officers (final-level enemies across episodes)

faction_indicators: Nazi German military hierarchy (guards, soldiers, dogs, officers)
Allied spy protagonist (William 'B.J.' Blazkowicz) opposing Nazi regime
Castle Wolfenstein as Nazi stronghold spatial marker
danger_signals: Heavily armed Nazi soldiers and guard dogs
Castle fortress architecture as hostile space
environmental_storytelling: Grid-based dungeon layout mimics prison/fortress architecture
Nazi bunker theming via visual assets and level design
Hidden rooms suggest clandestine intelligence operations
Weapon placement (knives to chain guns) reflects military escalation
